blogs.title: Building 3D Web Experiences with Three.js
blogs.excerpt: How I used Three.js and GSAP to create an immersive 3D portfolio with particle systems and orbital animations.
blogs.content:
# Building 3D Web Experiences with Three.js

When I decided to rebuild my portfolio, I wanted something that felt alive — not just a static page but an experience. That's when I discovered Three.js.

## Why Three.js?

Three.js makes WebGL accessible. Instead of writing raw shaders, you work with a clean API for scenes, cameras, geometries, and materials.

## The Orbital Scene

The hero section of my portfolio features a wireframe icosahedron surrounded by orbital rings and floating particles — all reacting to mouse movement.

```javascript
const icoGeo = new THREE.IcosahedronGeometry(1.5, 1);
const icoMat = new THREE.MeshBasicMaterial({
  color: 0xc8ff00,
  wireframe: true,
  transparent: true,
  opacity: 0.22,
});
const ico = new THREE.Mesh(icoGeo, icoMat);
scene.add(ico);
```

## Adding Interactivity

Mouse-driven parallax makes the scene feel responsive. I track the mouse position and smoothly interpolate the camera position.

## Performance Tips

- Use `requestAnimationFrame` for smooth 60fps
- Limit `pixelRatio` to 2 for high-DPI screens
- Keep polygon counts low for wireframes
- Use `BufferGeometry` for particle systems

The result? A portfolio that visitors actually want to explore.
blogs.author: Aryan Shakya
